stem: Karthik, a portfolio manager at Aegis Capital, intends to leave in six months to launch his own advisory firm after his employment ends. On personal time and with personal equipment, he drafts a business plan, designs marketing materials, and files company-registration paperwork with regulators. He has not contacted any Aegis client and has not informed Aegis of his plans, reasoning that only actual solicitation would harm the employer. Is Karthik's conduct most likely a violation of Standard IV(A)?

options.A: Not a violation, because mere preparation to enter a competing business is permitted so long as no clients are solicited and no firm property is used.
options.B: Violation, because failing to notify the employer of any competitive preparation is itself a breach of the duty of loyalty.
options.C: Violation, because formal regulatory registration of a competing firm while employed constitutes direct competition with the employer.

explanation:
First Principles Thinking: preparation versus competition while employed

A is correct. Standard IV(A) permits members and candidates to prepare to enter a competing business before termination, provided they do not solicit clients, do not use firm property, and continue to perform their current duties. Karthik's preparations are off-hours, use no firm resources, and involve no client contact, so the duty of loyalty remains intact.

B confuses the notification and consent rule that applies to rendering independent services during employment with purely preparatory activity. Notification is required when the member plans to provide services in an independent business while still employed, not for an after-employment venture that has not yet begun.

C misreads Handbook guidance in which the registration of a competing entity with regulators is expressly treated as acceptable preparation as long as no clients are pursued and the member's regular duties are fulfilled.

stem: Priya's employment with Harbor Asset Management is terminated effective 15 March. On 18 March, after her final pay and after she has ceased having firm responsibilities, she updates her LinkedIn profile to reflect her new role at a competing firm. LinkedIn automatically broadcasts the status change to her entire network, which includes several former Harbor clients. Did Priya most likely violate Standard IV(A)?

options.A: Violation, because the automated network notification to former Harbor clients amounts to implicit solicitation through a firm-adjacent channel.
options.B: Not a violation, because the profile update occurred after the employment relationship had ended and an automated status notification does not itself constitute solicitation.
options.C: Violation, because she should have removed all former Harbor clients from her LinkedIn connections before updating her profile.

explanation:
First Principles Thinking: end of the employment relationship and the meaning of solicitation

B is correct. The duty of loyalty extends only until the employment relationship ends, generally when the member is no longer paid and no longer has responsibilities at the firm. After that point a routine profile update, together with the automated network notification LinkedIn pushes out, does not by itself constitute solicitation under Standard IV(A).

A mistakes passive notification for active solicitation. Guidance on a substantially similar LinkedIn scenario concludes that an automatic network update is not the same as reaching out to induce business.

C imposes a requirement the standard does not contain. Members need not sever existing social network relationships upon departure; the Handbook's best practice recommendation is merely to maintain separate personal and professional accounts.

stem: Ortega, a senior analyst, has for three years maintained her own spreadsheets tracking client preferences, contact details, and meeting notes. She designed the structure herself, working partly on firm laptops and partly on her own laptop at home. Before resigning she copies the files to a personal drive, intending to use them only as templates and not to contact the clients. Which statement about her conduct is most accurate?

options.A: Not a violation, because the spreadsheet designs were her own creation and she will not use them to solicit clients.
options.B: Not a violation, because templates stripped of live client activity are not confidential firm records.
options.C: Violation, because employer records, whether hard copy, electronic, or stored on personal devices, and regardless of who prepared them, must not be taken without written permission.

explanation:
First Principles Thinking: employer ownership of work product

C is correct. Standard IV(A) treats work performed on behalf of the employer as firm property, even if the member personally created it and even if some of the work was done on personal devices. Client information, meeting notes, and derivative templates all qualify as employer records that must be returned or erased unless the firm gives written permission to retain them.

A is the most tempting distractor because intent matters in many ethics scenarios; here, however, the issue is ownership rather than future use. Taking the records is itself the violation.

B invents a 'templates only' exception the Handbook does not recognize. Any record prepared on behalf of the employer is off-limits absent consent.

stem: Four analysts planning to depart Cromwell Advisors in three weeks learn that a major Cromwell client has issued a request for proposal. The submission deadline falls before their resignation dates. Using only personal laptops and personal time, the group prepares a response on behalf of the new entity they intend to form after leaving, planning to submit it formally once they have resigned. Which statement is most accurate?

options.A: Not a violation, because they used no firm resources and will only formally submit the response after their employment has ended.
options.B: Not a violation, because the client openly invited competitive bids and the employees are merely responding to that public invitation.
options.C: Violation, because responding to the same RFP as their employer places them in direct competition while still employed, and they have not obtained the employer's permission.

explanation:
First Principles Thinking: direct competition during employment

C is correct. Handbook guidance is explicit: when a group of departing employees responds to an RFP to which their current employer is also responding, the resulting head-to-head competition violates Standard IV(A) unless the employees obtain permission from the employer. Personal time and personal equipment do not cure the competing-interest problem.

A conflates avoiding misuse of resources with satisfying the duty of loyalty. The breach here is the act of competing with the employer for the same business, not the tools used to compete.

B misuses the public-invitation framing. An RFP from a client is an opportunity for the employer; the duty of loyalty requires the employee to protect, rather than undermine, that opportunity while still employed.

stem: Eighteen months after leaving Vermeer Capital, where no nonsolicitation agreement was in place, Ostrom reconstructs a list of his former clients using publicly available regulatory filings and LinkedIn, without relying on any firm records. He then cold-calls each and offers the services of his new employer. Did Ostrom most likely violate Standard IV(A)?

options.A: Violation, because any contact with former clients of a prior firm implicitly exploits the prior employment relationship.
options.B: Violation, because the simple knowledge of which clients were served at Vermeer qualifies as confidential firm information.
options.C: Not a violation, because simple knowledge of former client identities is not confidential and public information may be used absent a restrictive agreement.

explanation:
First Principles Thinking: confidentiality boundaries after departure

C is correct. Once employment ends, the simple knowledge of the names and existence of former clients, as well as the former employee's general skills and experience, is not treated as confidential. Absent a nonsolicitation or similar agreement, the former employee may contact former clients using public information without breaching Standard IV(A).

A converts a general duty of loyalty into a lifetime no-contact rule that the standard does not impose.

B treats the mere existence of a client relationship as a trade secret, which the Handbook expressly rejects. It is the firm's records and client lists that are protected, not the bare identity of clients.

stem: Mejia, while still employed as an analyst at a registered investment company, registers a new advisory entity with state regulators on her own time. She does not solicit any clients, does not use firm data, and does not inform her employer of the registration. She intends to operate the new firm only after leaving. Which of the following is most accurate?

options.A: Not a violation, because the registration is a preparatory step taken on personal time and does not involve client solicitation or misuse of firm resources.
options.B: Violation, because forming a competing legal entity without notifying the employer is itself competitive activity conducted during employment.
options.C: Violation, because Standard IV(A) requires written employer consent for any step that could later enable competition.

explanation:
First Principles Thinking: preparatory steps versus competing activity

A is correct. Handbook guidance treats regulatory registration of a prospective competing firm as permissible preparation, provided the work is done on the member's own time, no firm resources are used, and no clients are solicited until employment has ended. Mejia's conduct fits squarely within that boundary.

B stretches the concept of 'competing activity' to include purely administrative preparation. Competition requires market-facing conduct such as soliciting clients or providing services; a dormant registration is not that.

C invents a blanket pre-consent rule. Notification and consent are required when the member will render services in an independent business while still employed, not for preparatory registration of a future venture.

stem: Nguyen, an adviser, has submitted her resignation and received employer permission to notify her clients of her departure. When a client asks why she is leaving, she explains, truthfully, that management has been pressuring advisers to recommend proprietary funds regardless of client needs, and she names the new firm she is joining. Her last day has not yet arrived. Which statement is most accurate?

options.A: Not a violation, because she has resigned, has employer permission to communicate with clients, and answers a direct question truthfully.
options.B: Violation, because disparaging the current employer to a client before the employment relationship has fully ended breaches Standard IV(A).
options.C: Not a violation, because whistle-blowing style disclosures intended to protect clients override the duty of loyalty.

explanation:
First Principles Thinking: continuation of loyalty duties after notice of resignation

B is correct. Submitting a resignation letter does not end the employment relationship. Loyalty obligations continue until pay and responsibilities cease. Disparaging the employer to its own clients during that window is a Standard IV(A) violation, even if every statement is literally true and even if naming the new firm was authorized. A member who believes practices are so egregious they harm clients must resign first and only then notify former clients of those concerns.

C is the seductive trap. Whistle-blowing protection permits limited action aimed at protecting clients or market integrity, but it does not authorize disparaging the employer to its own clients while the member is still on payroll.

A overreads the scope of 'permission to notify clients.' Permission to announce a departure does not authorize negative commentary about the employer's business practices.

stem: Chen reasonably believes her firm is illegally structuring transactions to inflate client fees in a manner that harms both clients and the integrity of the market. To preserve evidence, she copies internal records without authorization and later provides them to regulators. She does not share the records with competitors, does not use them for personal gain, and does not build any book of business from them. Is Chen's conduct most likely a violation of Standard IV(A)?

options.A: Not a violation, because whistle-blowing conduct aimed at protecting clients or the integrity of the market, rather than personal gain, is permitted.
options.B: Violation, because unauthorized copying of firm records is categorically prohibited under Standard IV(A).
options.C: Violation, because she was required to exhaust the firm's internal reporting channels before externally preserving any records.

explanation:
First Principles Thinking: the whistle-blowing exception to the duty of loyalty

A is correct. The Handbook recognizes that protecting the integrity of capital markets and the interests of clients is superior to the ordinary duty of loyalty. Conduct that would otherwise breach Standard IV(A), such as contradicting employer instructions or preserving firm records by copying them, can be justified when the clear intent is to protect clients or the market rather than to obtain personal benefit.

B treats the duty of loyalty as absolute and ignores the explicit whistle-blowing carve-out recognized by the Handbook.

C adds an exhaustion-of-internal-remedies requirement that Standard IV(A) does not impose. While members are encouraged to understand and use internal channels, the whistle-blowing justification does not require them to do so first.

stem: After tendering her resignation, Diaz is told by her employer that she may notify her clients of her departure. She sends each client a short note stating she will be leaving Meridian Advisors on 31 March and joining Ashton Partners. She provides no contact information at Ashton, describes none of Ashton's services, and invites no business. Which statement is most accurate?

options.A: Not a violation, because with employer permission she may identify her new employer so long as she neither promotes its services nor provides contact information.
options.B: Violation, because any reference to the new employer while still employed amounts to implicit solicitation of clients.
options.C: Not a violation, because once notice of resignation has been given, any client communication about future plans is permissible.

explanation:
First Principles Thinking: what may and may not be communicated during the notice period

A is correct. Once notice has been given, and subject to firm policy and permission, a member may inform clients of a departure and may identify the new employer, but must not provide new contact information, describe the new firm's services, or otherwise promote the new employer. Diaz's note stays strictly within those permitted disclosures and contains no promotional content.

B overstates the restriction by banning even the mention of the new employer. Handbook guidance expressly permits naming the new firm in this setting.

C understates the restriction by suggesting that giving notice unlocks unrestricted client communication, which it does not; promotional content and contact-information sharing remain off-limits.

stem: Liu, still employed as a wealth manager, privately accepts compensation from family friends to manage side portfolios using strategies similar to those offered by her employer. She works only after hours, uses no firm resources, and has sent her supervisor an email describing exactly what she is doing. She has received no response, and she begins rendering the services. Which statement is most accurate?

options.A: Not a violation, because her activities occur off-hours without using firm resources.
options.B: Not a violation, because her disclosure email to her supervisor satisfies her obligations under Standard IV(A).
options.C: Violation, because members who offer services similar to their employer's must not only notify the employer but also receive consent to the arrangement before rendering those services.

explanation:
First Principles Thinking: notification versus consent for independent services

C is correct. Standard IV(A) requires that a member who plans to render services in an independent business during employment both notify the employer of the types of services, the expected duration, and the compensation, and also receive the employer's consent to all of the terms before rendering any services. A unilateral email without a response does not constitute consent.

B collapses notification and consent into a single step. The Handbook treats them as distinct requirements, and silence from the employer cannot be read as approval.

A focuses on off-hours and resource use, which are relevant but insufficient. The competitive nature of the services is what triggers the consent requirement, regardless of time or equipment used.

stem: Kapoor is engaged as an independent contractor by a boutique firm under a written agreement strictly limiting her scope to validating credit-risk models. She subsequently contracts with a competing firm to provide unrelated strategy consulting, an activity not covered by her first agreement. The boutique is unaware of the second engagement. Which statement is most accurate?

options.A: Violation, because engaging with a competitor while contracted to the boutique is always a breach of loyalty.
options.B: Violation, because she was required to obtain the boutique's written consent before accepting any additional engagement.
options.C: Not a violation, because an independent contractor's duties under Standard IV(A) are governed by the terms of the written agreement, and the new engagement falls outside its defined scope.

explanation:
First Principles Thinking: nature of employment determines the duty owed

C is correct. For an independent contractor, Standard IV(A)'s duties are defined by the oral or written agreement between the contractor and the client. Because Kapoor's new engagement falls outside the services scoped in her first agreement, it does not breach a duty her first agreement did not impose.

A applies an employee-style blanket prohibition against competing, which does not fit an independent-contractor relationship where the duty is narrower and contract-defined.

B imports an employee-style pre-consent requirement that does not govern independent contractors, whose obligations run only to the terms of the agreement they have actually entered into.

stem: Banerjee, an equity analyst at a research firm, publishes a contrarian piece on his personal finance blog arguing that two specific stocks are mispriced. The firm's subscriber-only buy list, which Banerjee helped prepare, currently features one of the same stocks with supporting analysis that closely tracks his blog reasoning. He does not name the firm anywhere in the post. Which statement is most accurate?

options.A: Not a violation, because he writes in a personal capacity and his stated opinions differ in tone from the firm's official published view.
options.B: Violation, because he indirectly discloses the firm's confidential recommendations and uses firm-developed analysis for his personal purposes.
options.C: Not a violation, because the content of the internal buy list was not directly copied verbatim into his blog post.

explanation:
First Principles Thinking: indirect disclosure of confidential firm information

B is correct. Confidential firm research reaches the public indirectly when an analyst echoes the firm's paid-subscriber conclusions and underlying reasoning in a personal forum. The Handbook treats this indirect disclosure of firm analysis, together with the personal use of firm-developed insights, as a Standard IV(A) breach.

A hides behind the personal-capacity label. The violation is not that he holds an opinion but that the opinion reproduces proprietary firm analysis for the benefit of a public audience.

C draws a line at verbatim copying that the Handbook does not draw. Paraphrased, closely tracking analysis still conveys the same informational content and therefore the same misappropriation.

stem: Takahashi, a quantitative researcher, leaves a hedge fund and joins a competing firm. At the new firm she builds a new volatility-surface model using the statistical techniques and general analytical skills she refined at her previous employer. She takes no code, no documentation, no data, and no client information of any kind. Which statement is most accurate?

options.A: Not a violation, because the skills and experience gained while employed are not confidential and may be applied at a new employer.
options.B: Violation, because the analytical methodology she refined at her prior employer constitutes firm intellectual property.
options.C: Violation, because reusing any techniques learned at a prior firm requires its prior written permission.

explanation:
First Principles Thinking: experience is not confidential

A is correct. Once employment ends, the skills and experience obtained during that employment are expressly not treated as confidential or privileged. Standard IV(A) permits a former employee to apply those skills at a new employer so long as firm records, code, and documentation are not taken along with them.

B conflates generic analytical skill with proprietary models, code, or records. The Handbook protects the latter, not the former.

C invents a permission requirement for the use of one's own skills, which Standard IV(A) does not impose.

stem: Escobar, a relationship manager at Brighton Bank, has verbally accepted an offer at a competitor but has not yet submitted his resignation. Over lunch with his two largest clients, he mentions that he may soon be joining a firm with better alternative-investment access and suggests they 'stay in touch professionally.' Which statement is most accurate?

options.A: Not a violation, because casual social conversation is neither a formal pitch nor a request to transfer assets.
options.B: Violation, because he is implicitly soliciting clients of his current employer while still bound by the duty of loyalty.
options.C: Not a violation, because he did not explicitly request that the clients move their accounts.

explanation:
First Principles Thinking: implicit solicitation counts

B is correct. Standard IV(A) prohibits both explicit and implicit solicitation of an employer's clients while the member is still employed. Hinting at a superior offering at the new firm and inviting continued contact with key clients is precisely the implicit solicitation Handbook guidance captures.

A hides behind the 'casual' framing. The setting is immaterial; what matters is the communication's effect on the employer's client relationships.

C draws a bright line at explicit requests that the Handbook does not recognize. Implicit and explicit solicitation are treated equivalently for standard-of-conduct purposes.

stem: Zhao, a research associate, leaves the office early every Wednesday to care for a parent with a chronic illness. She discussed the arrangement openly with her manager, who agreed. Her research output remains at or above the team average, and she completes all of her core responsibilities. Which statement is most accurate?

options.A: Violation, because she is depriving her employer of the full advantage of her skills during regular working hours.
options.B: Not a violation, because Standard IV(A) does not require members to subordinate significant personal and family obligations to work when they have entered into a dialogue with the employer about balancing them.
options.C: Violation, because personal obligations cannot justify reduced availability regardless of resulting output.

explanation:
First Principles Thinking: scope of the loyalty duty

B is correct. Standard IV(A) is expressly not a blanket requirement to place employer interests ahead of important personal and family obligations. Members are encouraged to enter into a dialogue with their employer when personal matters may interfere with work, which is precisely what Zhao has done, while also maintaining her performance.

A treats loyalty as an absolute maximum-hours obligation. Handbook guidance rejects that framing.

C ignores the explicit carve-out in the standard for significant personal obligations when the member has discussed and arranged the situation with the employer.

stem: Thorne's employment contract expires at month-end, with no nonsolicitation agreement in place. Two days after his final paycheck and the formal end of his responsibilities, he calls several former clients whose names he remembers personally, without using any list or firm records, to announce his new affiliation. Which statement is most accurate?

options.A: Violation, because the simple knowledge of who the firm's clients were is itself confidential firm information.
options.B: Violation, because contacting former clients of a prior firm after departure is categorically prohibited under Standard IV(A).
options.C: Not a violation, because employment has ended, no firm records were used, and simple knowledge of client identities is not confidential absent a restrictive agreement.

explanation:
First Principles Thinking: post-employment contact with former clients

C is correct. After the employment relationship ends, simple knowledge of the names and existence of former clients is not confidential. With no nonsolicitation agreement in place and no use of firm records, Thorne is free to contact his former clients without breaching Standard IV(A).

A treats the identity of clients as a firm secret, a view the Handbook expressly rejects.

B invents a categorical post-employment ban that Standard IV(A) does not impose; the standard restricts the use of firm records and agreement-prohibited contact, not post-departure contact as such.

stem: Laroche maintains a firm-approved Twitter handle used for client engagement while employed at her wealth-management firm. Upon resigning, she retains full control of the handle and continues to post from it on behalf of her new employer, arguing that her followers chose to follow her personally. Which statement is most accurate?

options.A: Violation, because firm-approved business-related social-media accounts are firm property and must be transferred or deleted as directed by the firm's policies.
options.B: Not a violation, because social-media followers voluntarily chose to associate with her as an individual rather than with her firm.
options.C: Not a violation, because she has simply updated where she is posting from, which is analogous to a permitted personal LinkedIn profile update.

explanation:
First Principles Thinking: firm-approved accounts are firm assets

A is correct. Firm-approved, business-related social-media accounts are part of the firm's assets. Departing members must transfer or delete such accounts as directed by firm policy. Laroche's retention and reuse of the handle for a new employer misappropriates firm property in violation of Standard IV(A).

B treats followers as a personal asset. The Handbook treats firm-sponsored channels and their audiences as business assets of the firm, not of the individual who managed them.

C confuses a personal LinkedIn profile update, which the Handbook permits after departure, with continued use of a firm-branded account, which it does not.

stem: Andersson, still employed as a portfolio analyst, uses a vacation day to interview at a competitor, prepares her resume on personal time, and accepts a contingent offer. She has not told any colleague or client, has used no firm data, and continues to complete all of her current responsibilities. Which statement is most accurate?

options.A: Not a violation, because job-search activities conducted on personal time, without client contact or use of firm property, do not breach the duty of loyalty.
options.B: Violation, because simply interviewing with a competitor while employed is itself a competitive act inconsistent with loyalty.
options.C: Violation, because she should have obtained her current employer's permission before interviewing with a competing firm.

explanation:
First Principles Thinking: boundary between preparation and competition

A is correct. Standard IV(A) permits members to prepare for a new role, including interviewing, updating resumes, and accepting contingent offers, so long as they continue fulfilling their current responsibilities and do not solicit clients or use firm property. Andersson's conduct remains on the preparation side of that line.

B treats interviewing itself as competitive activity, which Handbook guidance does not. The prohibited act is actual competition with or solicitation from the employer, not the search for new employment.

C invents a permission requirement that would effectively prevent any employee from seeking new work, which no part of Standard IV(A) contemplates.

stem: Tanaka, a senior quantitative analyst, personally developed a suite of asset-allocation models at her employer over five years, writing every line of code herself on firm equipment. On her last day she uploads the complete code base to a personal cloud drive, believing the code is hers because she wrote it. She does not take any client data. Which statement is most accurate?

options.A: Not a violation, because she personally authored the code and is taking no client information.
options.B: Violation, because work product created on behalf of the employer is firm property regardless of who authored it and may not be taken without written permission.
options.C: Not a violation, because she intends to use the code only for personal reference rather than for commercial benefit.

explanation:
First Principles Thinking: authorship does not defeat firm ownership

B is correct. Work performed on behalf of an employer, including models and code the member personally authored, belongs to the employer and must not be taken upon departure without written permission. Tanaka's self-authorship is irrelevant to ownership, and her use of firm equipment only reinforces the point.

A reflects a common intuition that authors own their work, but within an employment context Handbook guidance squarely treats such work product as the employer's property.

C imports an intent test the standard does not apply. The misappropriation itself is the violation, regardless of planned future use.

stem: Vargas's resignation from his advisory firm takes effect on 30 April; he has received his final paycheck and has no remaining responsibilities. On 2 May he contacts several former clients using information he obtained from publicly available regulatory filings rather than from any records of his former employer. There is no nonsolicitation agreement in place. Which statement is most accurate?

options.A: Violation, because a reasonable cooling-off period must elapse before any post-departure contact with clients of a prior employer.
options.B: Violation, because any contact with clients of a former employer is prohibited under Standard IV(A).
options.C: Not a violation, because his employment has ended, he is using public information, and no nonsolicitation agreement restricts him.

explanation:
First Principles Thinking: post-employment freedom absent contractual restriction

C is correct. Once employment ends, Standard IV(A) does not prohibit contacting former clients, provided the contact information is derived from public sources rather than firm records and no applicable agreement forbids such contact. Vargas satisfies each of these conditions.

A invents a cooling-off period that the standard does not contain.

B imposes a categorical post-employment ban that the Handbook explicitly rejects; the restriction runs to the use of firm records and to agreement-based prohibitions, not to post-departure contact per se.

stem: Harper's firm has a written policy permitting advisers to connect with clients on LinkedIn, subject to compliance review of any content that references investment ideas. Harper follows the policy, uses a profile clearly labeled as an employee of the firm, connects with existing clients, and posts only content that compliance has pre-approved. Which statement is most accurate?

options.A: Violation, because intermingling personal and professional social-media connections always creates the appearance of solicitation.
options.B: Violation, because LinkedIn connections with clients are inherently incompatible with the duty of loyalty.
options.C: Not a violation, because she is abiding by her firm's written social-media policies and posting only firm-approved content.

explanation:
First Principles Thinking: compliance with firm social-media policy

C is correct. Standard IV(A) requires members to understand and abide by their firm's policies on the acceptable use of social media for client engagement. Harper is doing exactly that: her engagement is authorized, her content is pre-approved, and her profile accurately identifies her current employer.

A overstates the intermingling concern. Maintaining separate personal and professional accounts is a best practice, but a firm-authorized professional presence that follows firm policy is not itself a violation.

B treats client connections as per se improper, which the Handbook does not; firm-approved engagement on social platforms is expressly contemplated as a legitimate business activity.
